goal: Verify and secure the server-to-server auth path used by bluepilot when calling maya-core Block-2 memory.
scope_out: Do not copy or fork maya-core auth logic.
Do not change bluepilot's local JSON fallback.
Do not bind persona, voice, or ethics in this task.
Do not allow bluepilot to write confirmed memory directly.
claims: maya-core /api/maya/memory accepts the configured gate token for server-to-server GET and POST.
maya-core /api/maya/memory continues to accept existing Maya session auth.
missing or wrong server-to-server tokens remain blocked when no valid Maya session is present.
bluepilot's existing remote client header matches the accepted maya-core auth path.
assumptions: maya-core's builder gate auth is the canonical server-to-server auth helper for MAYA_CORE_GATE_TOKEN and MAYA_BUILDER_GATE_TOKEN.
bluepilot already sends x-maya-core-gate-token from MAYA_CORE_GATE_TOKEN or MAYA_BUILDER_GATE_TOKEN.
The BP-124 implementation may touch maya-core files outside the bluepilot repository, with evidence recorded in the review packet.
dod: maya-core memory route uses existing builder gate auth instead of duplicating token checks.
tests cover valid gate token, missing/wrong token rejection, and existing Maya auth fallback for GET and POST.
docs/BP_C3_MAYA_MEMORY_CONTRACT_v0.md states that maya-core memory accepts the shared gate auth path.
review-packets/BP-124.md records external maya-core changes and verification evidence.
git diff --check exits 0 in both touched repos.
stop_conditions: A second auth implementation becomes necessary.
Changing bluepilot runtime memory behavior becomes necessary.
Persona, voice, or ethics binding becomes necessary.
Existing Maya session auth would be weakened.
prior_task_findings: BP-123 sends x-maya-core-gate-token, but maya-core memory still used the normal Maya request auth directly. Real deployment requires the memory route to accept the existing server-to-server gate token path.
